questions.scenario: Before reading a config file, your script must verify it actually exists.
questions.question: Use [[ -f ]] to check if /home/student/logs/app.log exists and print 'exists' or 'missing'.
questions.description: [[ -f path ]] tests for a regular file. -d for directory, -e for anything (file or dir), -s for non-empty file, -x for executable. [[ ]] is the modern bash test command — prefer it over [ ] or test.
use_cases: Check backup files exist before declaring success
Guard script logic on file presence
hints: [[ -f path ]] returns true if path is a regular file

questions.scenario: A script needs to create a directory only if it doesn't already exist.
questions.question: Use [[ -d ]] to check if /tmp/reactor exists and create it only if it doesn't.
questions.description: -d tests for a directory. The ! negates the test. [[ ! -d path ]] is true when the directory does NOT exist. This pattern is cleaner than mkdir -p for cases where you need to branch on existence.
use_cases: Idempotent directory creation in deploy scripts
Guard setup steps that must only run once
Detect missing required directories at startup
hints: [[ ! -d path ]] is true if directory does NOT exist

questions.scenario: A script receives a SERVICE variable. Before using it, verify it's not empty.
questions.question: Set SERVICE='' and use [[ -z ]] to detect the empty string, printing a warning.
questions.description: -z tests for an empty (zero-length) string. -n tests for non-empty. These are the standard string emptiness checks. Always quote the variable: [[ -z "$var" ]] not [[ -z $var ]].
use_cases: Check that a variable was set before use
Guard against empty database query results
hints: Run: SERVICE='' && [[ -z "$SERVICE" ]] && echo 'WARNING: SERVICE is empty'

questions.scenario: A script compares two version strings and must branch based on equality.
questions.question: Set VERSION='2.1.0' and check if it equals '2.1.0' using [[ == ]], printing 'match' or 'no match'.
questions.description: [[ == ]] does string comparison. [[ != ]] for inequality. The right side can be a glob pattern (without quotes): [[ $var == *.log ]] matches files ending in .log. For regex use =~ instead.

questions.scenario: A script validates that a service name contains only alphanumeric characters and hyphens — no special chars.
questions.question: Use [[ =~ ]] to test if 'nginx-proxy' matches the pattern ^[a-zA-Z0-9-]+$ and print the result.
questions.description: [[ string =~ regex ]] performs POSIX extended regex matching. Only available in [[ ]], NOT in [ ]. The match is unanchored by default — use ^ and $ to anchor. BASH_REMATCH holds capture groups.
use_cases: Extract data with regex capture groups
Sanitise input before using it in commands

questions.scenario: Show the key difference between [ ] and [[ ]]: regex matching only works in one.
questions.question: Show that [[ 'abc123' =~ ^[a-z]+[0-9]+$ ]] works but [ 'abc123' =~ ... ] would fail.
questions.description: [ ] is POSIX sh, doesn't support =~, <, >, or pattern matching. [[ ]] is bash-specific and supports =~, regex, globbing on the right side, and doesn't word-split or glob-expand variables. Always use [[ ]] in bash scripts.
use_cases: Understand why scripts fail in sh vs bash
Use [[ ]] consistently to avoid subtle bugs
